question: A modification command terminates an embedded subprocess that contains the only active task in the root process instance. After the command applies, what is the resulting state?

options.text: The subprocess is terminated; the process instance keeps waiting for new instructions.
The subprocess and its inner task are terminated, and the process instance is also terminated because no active element instances remain.
The command is rejected because terminating the only active scope would end the process instance.
The subprocess is terminated and a fresh instance of it is automatically created to keep the process alive.

a.text: A process instance with no active element instances cannot wait — the engine ends it as part of the documented termination cascade.

b.text: Per the docs, terminating a subprocess terminates all active instances inside it, and if the terminating element was the last active instance of the process instance, the process instance itself is also terminated.

c.text: The rejection rule for ending the last active element applies only to child process instances created by a call activity, not to the root process instance.

d.text: There is no automatic recreation; the engine simply applies the documented cascade and ends the process.

explanation: The docs spell out the termination cascades: terminating a subprocess terminates all active inner instances; terminating the last active instance inside a subprocess terminates the subprocess; terminating the last active instance of the process instance terminates the process. Here, all three rules fire in sequence, so the root process instance ends.
